text: All right that's like 24 of the time. All right welcome to my talk. I'm
presenting Crowdsourcing Research Synthesis on AT Pro: Envisioning an
Inclusive Future. And I'm Jay Patel from the University of Maryland. So I want to
preface this by the motivation for this lightning talk. Back in summer 2025 I
was doing my first systematic review. So who's familiar with the idea of a
systematic review in research? Okay who's familiar with the idea of any kind of
review research synthesis? Very good okay. So a systematic review is a very
systematic way of sort of assessing how much evidence there is out there in the
broader research literature. And that involves four key steps which is
searching for relevant preprints and papers through keyword searches and
natural language searches. Screening for relevant reports so from all the papers
that you search for, which ones are relevant to your questions, and appraising
or evaluating the study quality. After appraising for study quality you might
drop off filter out a few more articles. And then when you do that you'll then
synthesize the evidence that you have into some sort of prose which is suitable for
publication. So this is what I've been working on for the past year. And the
question that I was really trying to answer was how suitable are large language
models for assessing research reports. So as I was doing this I noticed a few
recurring problems that are not exactly novel but I really felt them in my bones.
One is just that it's very slow to do this. It takes about one to two days to read a
paper very deeply and to be able to say something smart about that. There are many
such citations for that claim. It's also very tedious after you learn it, after you go to the
first dozen or so papers. And if you want evidence of that you can just go to me
because I've done this you know hundreds of times. This is actually a very costly
procedure. So if you have a trained graduate student and maybe one other
person working on it, it takes quite a lot of money. And there are only a few
citations for this sort of thing but estimates range between fifty and a hundred
and forty one thousand dollars USD. Actually in pharmacology or pharma companies it'll cost
about a hundred and forty one thousand per systematic review. So that's quite a lot.
You know and sadly this is also an error prone sort of thing. Ninety three percent of systematic
reviews have at least one error in the search process. About fifty nine percent of the sampled
ones have at least one error in the extraction process extracting key details of methods and analyses.
And then when it comes to appraisal or evaluating the study quality, well there are
very many problems and I would call that foobar or I guess you know that acronym.
And then there's also a number of other problems like you use very often small
homogeneous teams which limits the quality of your systematic review. Ideally use
very large heterogeneous teams so they could get through the work more quickly. Tackle the
problem from multiple frames using different kinds of knowledge, different kinds of skills,
and use different connections to make sure you have a really great product at the end of
it.
And even more problematically, many of these systematic reviews because they take so long
are dead upon arrival. They're rarely updated after publication and very troublesome to maintain
even when you have a fancy sort of living web page. So what we would ideally have is you know solutions
to these four key problems, volume and velocity. We would increase the speed at which we do
reviews so that they take weeks not years. We would improve the veracity or accuracy and I propose
we do that with crowdsourcing, increasing the team size and ensuring that certain team members are always
engaged with error detection and error correction. We would hopefully increase the diversity of the different
perspectives and contributors on our teams. That would allow us to reframe things, make suggestions, make
interesting connections and then also have living systematic reviews which helps with providing timely data to
people who might benefit from that data. And we kind of do this thing already with open source software
development right. So how do we do this? Do we use some kind of shiny app with R, open science
repos? Do we use like journal specific tools or journalware? This question has been asked on Blue Sky
actually quite recently by this user who asked what is the best platform to host something like this?
Meaning a living systematic review. Is it shiny? Is it a website? Well you know one other user had this
interesting idea of a wiki which is a more dynamic approach where multiple researchers can edit and attach new evidence and reviewers
and then various other people can make approvals, rejections and modifications. So something like Wikipedia but with additional guardrails.
The nice thing is that the ATProto system is kind of like a full-ish stack right now. We have all the
sort of necessary tooling to do an MVP. So suppose you have a bunch of research posters, a bunch of preprints, a bunch of fully
polished papers. And then you sort of discourse graph them with their questions, claims and evidence.
And push those data into symbol collections and margin annotations. Then you could probably layer
that on to some kind of UI where users can validate the different extracted discourse graph nodes.
Agree and then publish that into a leaflet blog post which recently has been working on
creating sort of living documents, living posts. So if we do this, you know, I think the near-term
goal could be to leverage the existing apps, create the MVP. Hopefully that could be done within a few
weeks to months. In the medium term, I think it'd be cool to query data across multiple crowdsource reviews
and scaffold also what's called primary research which would be not reviews but primary experiments,
surveys, surveys, interviews. And in the very long term, it'd be very nice if we could build
interesting visual dashboards and data stories that would appeal more to the public, more for public
engagement rather than just creating, you know, typical research reports for scientists. So a fair number of
things that we can do and I'd like you to kind of get your help with this. So if you're a builder,
can you help me build the missing pieces? If you're a scholar or someone else, you know, can you help me
test drive the MVP, spread the word? And I don't know who else is out here except a builder or a scholar or
advocate but anyone else, you know, you can also help test drive the MVP, spread the word, get involved.
I would really kind of love your advice here. I'd like to thank my research mentor Joel Chan for
conceptual guidance here and then Ronan for also motivating this talk and bringing me out here.
And finally, I'm available on ORCID and Blue Sky. Thank you.
Thank you, Jay. Questions, anyone? Maybe I'll ask a question about the, yeah, just comparison,
like what's your sort of mental model for where app proto fits in versus like AI? How do you see the
two combining? I'm agreeable to both approaches. I haven't thought about AI as much because I think
simply increasing the team size would be such a great benefit to
the problem at hand. I know that for systematic reviews, there's an incredible amount of work
being done right now for screening papers, extracting bits of questions, claims, and evidence,
and also doing the synthesis. I agree more for using it for search,
screening, and extracting questions, claims, and evidence. A little bit less so for synthesis because
that's way more complicated and way more fun. As an amateur, I've tried to sort of do that myself.
And it's not been as successful. But certainly, I think, you know, search, in fact, I couldn't have done
part of my systematic review without using some AI-assisted elements into my workflow. It really
would not have been possible. So I strongly advocate for using the right AI programs to help with search
and screening, although my systematic review is so small that I can do the screening myself manually.
And then when it comes to appraisal, I have a certain way of doing it that I like. And so I feel
comfortable just doing that myself. But I'm open to anyone who might be able to sort of automate custom-built
checklists and things using some sort of AI agent. So that is, yeah, it's quite doable. Cool. Anyone else?
You mentioned using high-quality LLMs. Did you benchmark maybe like medium-level or low-quality ones?
I did not do the, for the systematic review that I was conducting, I did not do the benchmarking myself.
I was merely reviewing the studies that others had done, the benchmarking experiments that others have
done. And so what I noted from that, which is actually helpful for this sort of meta project,
is that, you know, a smattering of results, the more you focus these foundation models, the better they do.
Using sort of, you know, fine-tuned data, better prompts, better context engineering,
those things all sorts of help. But I think none of these models achieve, you know, expert human
performance on every trial. So it would really be beneficial to have humans and AI agents work
together across the systematic review process. Certainly, I see them being very useful for appraisal,
right? Let's say you have a long checklist that's useful for evaluating the methods of analyses.
You should, you should consider certain LLMs, I think. That's perfectly fine. Yeah.
Any more questions? Maybe a quick one while Sophie sets up or we'll just move to the next talk.
Okay. Well, let's thank Jay again.
